([/əˈpɪfəni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*ə-PIF-ə-nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), also known as **Theophany** in [Eastern Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Christian "Eastern Christian") tradition,[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a Christian [feast day](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") commemorating the [visit of the Magi](/wiki/Visit_of_the_Magi "Visit of the Magi"), the [baptism of Jesus](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus"), and the [wedding at Cana](/wiki/Wedding_at_Cana "Wedding at Cana").[[6]](#cite_note-CathIre-6)

Lutheran priest and altar servers at [Saint Mary's Church](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Church,_Sigtuna "St. Mary's Church, Sigtuna") in [Sigtuna](/wiki/Sigtuna "Sigtuna"), Sweden, standing in the [narthex](/wiki/Narthex "Narthex") during [recessional hymn](/wiki/Processional_hymn#Recessional_hymn "Processional hymn") at the end of Epiphany Day [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)")

**Epiphany** ([/əˈpɪfəni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*ə-PIF-ə-nee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), also known as **Theophany** in [Eastern Christian](/wiki/Eastern_Christian "Eastern Christian") tradition,[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a Christian [feast day](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") commemorating the [visit of the Magi](/wiki/Visit_of_the_Magi "Visit of the Magi"), the [baptism of Jesus](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus"), and the [wedding at Cana](/wiki/Wedding_at_Cana "Wedding at Cana").[[6]](#cite_note-CathIre-6)

In [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity"), the feast commemorates principally (but not solely) the visit of the Magi to the [Christ Child](/wiki/Christ_Child "Christ Child"), and thus Jesus Christ's physical manifestation to the [Gentiles](/wiki/Gentile "Gentile").[[7]](#cite_note-Packer1893-7)[[8]](#cite_note-SCJ-8) It is sometimes called **Three Kings' Day**, and in some traditions celebrated as [Little Christmas](/wiki/Little_Christmas "Little Christmas").[[9]](#cite_note-Heiligman2007-9) Moreover, the feast of the Epiphany, in some [denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination"), also initiates the liturgical season of [Epiphanytide](/wiki/Epiphanytide "Epiphanytide").[[10]](#cite_note-UMPH-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[Eastern Christians](/wiki/Eastern_Christians "Eastern Christians"), on the other hand, commemorate the baptism of Jesus in the [River Jordan](/wiki/River_Jordan "River Jordan"), seen as his manifestation to the world as the [Son of God](/wiki/Son_of_God_(Christianity) "Son of God (Christianity)"),[[6]](#cite_note-CathIre-6) and celebrate it as the Feast of the Epiphany or of the [Theophany](/wiki/Theophany "Theophany"). The traditional site of the ministry of [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist") is in [Al-Maghtas](/wiki/Al-Maghtas "Al-Maghtas") in [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), with the baptism of Jesus once marked in [Byzantine times](/wiki/Timeline_of_Palestine#Byzantine_period "Timeline of Palestine") by a cross in the middle of the Jordan River, between the Jordanian site and [Qasr al-Yahud](/wiki/Qasr_al-Yahud "Qasr al-Yahud") in the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank").[[12]](#cite_note-unescobof-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The traditional date for the feast is January 6. However, since 1970 the celebration has been held in some countries on the Sunday after January 1. Those Eastern Churches that are still following the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar") observe the feast on what, according to the internationally used [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar"), is 19 January,[[14]](#cite_note-14) because of the current 13-day difference between the Julian and Gregorian calendars.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The [Alawites](/wiki/Alawites "Alawites") and the [Middle Eastern Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_Middle_East "Christianity in the Middle East") also observe the feast on January 19.[[2]](#cite_note-antakya1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-antakya2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-antakya3-4)

In many Western Churches, the [eve of the feast](/wiki/Vigil_(liturgy) "Vigil (liturgy)") is celebrated as [Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night_(holiday) "Twelfth Night (holiday)") (Epiphany Eve) on January 5.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) The Monday after Epiphany is known as [Plough Monday](/wiki/Plough_Monday "Plough Monday").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Popular Epiphany customs include [Epiphany singing](/wiki/Star_singers "Star singers"), [chalking the door](/wiki/Chalking_the_door "Chalking the door"), [having one's house blessed](/wiki/House_blessing "House blessing"), consuming [Three Kings Cake](/wiki/Three_Kings_Cake "Three Kings Cake"), [winter swimming](/wiki/Winter_swimming "Winter swimming"), as well as attending [church services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service").[[19]](#cite_note-PolonCantwell1983-19) It is customary for Christians in many localities to remove their [Christmas decorations](/wiki/Christmas_decoration "Christmas decoration") on Epiphany Eve (Twelfth Night),[[20]](#cite_note-Learning-20) although those in other [Christian countries](/wiki/Christian_state "Christian state") historically remove them on [Candlemas](/wiki/Candlemas "Candlemas"), the conclusion of Epiphanytide.[[21]](#cite_note-McGregor2023-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Edworthy2008-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Roud2008-23) According to one seventeenth-century tradition, it is inauspicious to remove Christmas decorations before Epiphany Eve and those who do not remove them on that date have the opportunity to take them down on Candlemas.[[21]](#cite_note-McGregor2023-21)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_(holiday)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word Epiphany is from [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek") ἐπιφάνεια, *epipháneia*, meaning manifestation or appearance. It is derived from the verb φαίνειν, *phainein*, meaning 'to appear'.[[26]](#cite_note-26) In [classical Greek](/wiki/Classical_Greek "Classical Greek") it was used for the appearance of dawn, of an enemy in war, but especially of a manifestation of a deity to a worshipper (a [theophany](/wiki/Theophany "Theophany")). In the [Septuagint](/wiki/Septuagint "Septuagint") the word is used of a manifestation of the God of Israel ([2 Maccabees 15:27](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=2%20Maccabees%2015:27&version=nrsvae)).[[27]](#cite_note-Blue-27) In the New Testament the word is used in [2 Timothy 1:10](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=2%20Timothy%201:10&version=nrsv) to refer either to the [birth of Christ](/wiki/Birth_of_Christ "Birth of Christ") or to his appearance after his resurrection, and five times to refer to his [Second Coming](/wiki/Second_Coming "Second Coming").[[27]](#cite_note-Blue-27)

Alternative names for the feast in [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") include τα Θεοφάνια (*ta Theophánia*; 'Theophany'; a neuter plural rather than feminine singular), η Ημέρα των Φώτων (*i Iméra ton Fóton*; 'The Day of the Lights'; modern Greek pronunciation), and τα Φώτα (*ta Fóta*, 'The Lights').[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), it is called *Eid al-Ghitas* ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): عيد الغِطاس).[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_(holiday)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_(holiday)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Magi_%281%29.jpg/250px-Magi_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Magi_(1).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/7222_Adoraci%C3%B3n_de_los_Reyes_Magos.jpg/250px-7222_Adoraci%C3%B3n_de_los_Reyes_Magos.jpg)](/wiki/File:7222_Adoraci%C3%B3n_de_los_Reyes_Magos.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Bartolom%C3%A9_Esteban_Murillo_-_Adoration_of_the_Magi_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Bartolom%C3%A9_Esteban_Murillo_-_Adoration_of_the_Magi_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bartolom%C3%A9_Esteban_Murillo_-_Adoration_of_the_Magi_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Adoration of the Magi* by [Bartolomé Esteban Murillo](/wiki/Bartolom%C3%A9_Esteban_Murillo "Bartolomé Esteban Murillo") (17th century)

Epiphany may have originated in the Greek-speaking eastern half of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") as a feast to honor the [baptism of Jesus](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus"). Around 200, the theologian [Clement of Alexandria](/wiki/Clement_of_Alexandria "Clement of Alexandria") wrote:

> "But the followers of [the early Christian [Gnostic](/wiki/Gnostic "Gnostic") teacher] Basilides celebrate the day of His Baptism too, spending the previous night in readings. And they say that it was the 15th of the month [Tybi](/wiki/Tybi "Tybi") of the 15th year of [Tiberius Caesar](/wiki/Tiberius_Caesar "Tiberius Caesar"). And some say that it was observed the 11th of the same month."

— Clement of Alexandria (c. AD 200)

The Egyptian dates given correspond to January 6 and 10.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartindale1909-30) The followers of [Basilides](/wiki/Basilides "Basilides") were a Gnostic sect.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The reference to "readings" suggests that the Basilides were reading the [Gospels](/wiki/Gospels "Gospels"). In ancient [gospel manuscripts](/wiki/Gospel_manuscripts "Gospel manuscripts"), the text is arranged to indicate passages for liturgical readings. If a congregation began reading [Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark") at the beginning of the year, it might arrive at the story of the Baptism on January 6, thus explaining the date of the feast.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) If Christians read Mark in the same format the Basilides did, the two groups could have arrived at the January 6 date independently.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The earliest reference to Epiphany as a Christian feast was in AD 361, by [Ammianus Marcellinus](/wiki/Ammianus_Marcellinus "Ammianus Marcellinus").[[34]](#cite_note-34) The holiday is listed twice, which suggests a double feast of baptism and birth.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartindale1909-30) The baptism of Jesus was originally assigned to the same date as the birth because [Luke 3:23](https://www.esv.org/Luke+3:23) was read to mean that Jesus was exactly 30 when he was baptized; it is said by many [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers") that Jesus was the age of 30, although not necessarily exactly that age.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

[Epiphanius of Salamis](/wiki/Epiphanius_of_Salamis "Epiphanius of Salamis") says that January 6 is Christ's "Birthday; that is, His Epiphany" (*hemera genethlion toutestin epiphanion*).[[36]](#cite_note-36) He also asserts that the [Wedding at Cana](/wiki/Wedding_at_Cana "Wedding at Cana") occurred on the same calendar day.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEpiphaniuschapters_xxviii_and_xxix_P.G.,_XLI,_940_sq.-37) Epiphanius assigns the Baptism to November 6.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartindale1909-30)

The scope to Epiphany expanded to include the commemoration of his birth; the visit of the [Magi](/wiki/Biblical_Magi "Biblical Magi"), all of Jesus' childhood events, up to and including the Baptism by [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"); and even the miracle at the wedding at Cana in [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee").[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin")-speaking West, the holiday emphasized the visit of the Magi. The Magi represented the non-Jewish peoples of the world, so this was considered a "revelation to the gentiles".[[39]](#cite_note-39) In this event, Christian writers also inferred a revelation to the [Israelites](/wiki/Israelites "Israelites"). [John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom") identified the significance of the meeting between the Magi and [Herod](/wiki/Herod_the_Great "Herod the Great")'s court:

> "The star had been hidden from them so that, on finding themselves without their guide, they would have no alternative but to consult the Jews. In this way the birth of Jesus would be made known to all."[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Even before 354,[[41]](#cite_note-Pokhilko-41) the Western Church had separated the celebration of the Nativity of Christ as the feast of [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") and set its date as December 25; it reserved January 6 as a commemoration of the manifestation of Christ, especially to the Magi, but also at his baptism and at the wedding feast of Cana.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In 385, the pilgrim [Egeria](/wiki/Egeria_(pilgrim) "Egeria (pilgrim)") (also known as Silvia) described a celebration in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"), which she called *Epiphany* that commemorated the Nativity.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Even at this early date, there was an [octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgy) "Octave (liturgy)") associated with the feast. The Georgian Chantbook of Jerusalem compiled in the mid 6th century contains hymns for this feast[[44]](#cite_note-44) that were likely written between the time of Egeria and the turn of the 6th century.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

In a sermon delivered on December 25, 380, St. [Gregory of Nazianzus](/wiki/Gregory_of_Nazianzus "Gregory of Nazianzus") referred to the day as *the Theophany* (*ta theophania*, formerly the name of a pagan festival at [Delphi](/wiki/Delphi "Delphi")),[[46]](#cite_note-46) saying expressly that it is a day commemorating "the holy nativity of Christ" and told his listeners that they would soon be celebrating the baptism of Christ.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMigne1858312Oratio_XXXVIII-47) Then, on January 6 and 7, he preached two more sermons,[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMigne1858333Oratio_XXXIX_&_XL-48) in which he declared that the celebration of the birth of Christ and the visitation of the Magi had already taken place, and that they would now commemorate his Baptism.[[49]](#cite_note-49) At this time, celebration of the two events was beginning to be observed on separate occasions, at least in [Cappadocia](/wiki/Cappadocia "Cappadocia").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

St. [John Cassian](/wiki/John_Cassian "John Cassian") says that even in his time (beginning of the 5th century), [Egyptian](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church") [monasteries](/wiki/Monasteries "Monasteries") celebrated the Nativity and the Baptism together on January 6.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The [Armenian Apostolic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church") continues to celebrate January 6 as the only commemoration of the Nativity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_(holiday)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Epifania_del_Tallero_02.jpg/250px-Epifania_del_Tallero_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Epifania_del_Tallero_02.jpg)

Epiphany celebration in [Gemona del Friuli](/wiki/Gemona_del_Friuli "Gemona del Friuli"), Italy

In the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church"), Epiphany is traditionally celebrated with an [octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgy) "Octave (liturgy)"), beginning on January 6 and ending on January 13. From 1893 until 1955 the Sunday within that octave had been the feast of the [Holy Family](/wiki/Holy_Family "Holy Family"), and Christmastide was reckoned as the twelve days ending on January 5, followed by the January 6–13 octave. Before the 1969 revision of its liturgy, the Sundays following the Octave of Epiphany or, when this was abolished, following the Feast of the [Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Baptism_of_the_Lord "Baptism of the Lord"), which was instituted to take the place of the Octave of Epiphany were named as the "Second (etc., up to Sixth) Sunday after Epiphany", as the at least 24 Sundays following [Pentecost Sunday](/wiki/Pentecost_Sunday "Pentecost Sunday") and [Trinity Sunday](/wiki/Trinity_Sunday "Trinity Sunday") were known as the "Second (etc.) Sunday after Pentecost". (If a year had more than 24 Sundays after Pentecost, up to four unused post-Epiphany Sundays were inserted between the 23rd and the 24th Sunday after Pentecost.) The *Encyclopedia of Catholic Devotions and Practices*, which received the [imprimatur](/wiki/Imprimatur "Imprimatur") of [John Michael D'Arcy](/wiki/John_Michael_D%27Arcy "John Michael D'Arcy"), with reference to Epiphanytide, thus states that "the Epiphany season extends from January 6 to [Septuagesima Sunday](/wiki/Septuagesima_Sunday "Septuagesima Sunday"), and has from one to six Sundays, according to the date of Easter. White is the color for the octave; green is the [liturgical color](/wiki/Liturgical_colours "Liturgical colours") for the season."[[51]](#cite_note-Ball2003-51)

In 1955, [Pope Pius XII](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII") abolished all but three liturgical octaves, thus deleting that of Epiphany, and the 1969 revision of the [General Roman Calendar](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar "General Roman Calendar") instituted the current system which makes the date variable to some extent.[[52]](#cite_note-52) It also made the Feast of the Epiphany part of [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide"), which it defined as extending from the First [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") of Christmas (the evening of December 24) up to and including the Sunday after Epiphany (the Sunday after January 6).[[53]](#cite_note-UNLY33-53) In 1955 a separate [feast of the Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Baptism_of_the_Lord "Feast of the Baptism of the Lord") was also instituted, thus weakening further the connection in the West between the feast of the Epiphany and the commemoration of the baptism of Christ. ([Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians"), in an apparent reference to baptism, refer to the January 6 celebration as *Vízkereszt*, a term that recalls the words *víz* ('water') and *kereszt* or *kereszt-ség* ('baptism').)

Prior to 1976, [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") churches also observed an octave beginning on January 6.

## Epiphany by Christian tradition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_(holiday)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Epiphany by Christian tradition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Lstibo%C5%99%2C_detail_dve%C5%99%C3%AD_fary.JPG/250px-Lstibo%C5%99%2C_detail_dve%C5%99%C3%AD_fary.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lstibo%C5%99,_detail_dve%C5%99%C3%AD_fary.JPG)

*K † M † B † 2009* written on a door of a rectory in a [Czech](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") village, to bless the house by Christ. Christians of various denominations, including Catholics, [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutherans "Lutherans") and Anglicans practice the [chalking the door](/wiki/Chalking_the_door "Chalking the door") on Epiphanytide.

Epiphany is celebrated by both the Eastern and Western Churches, but a major difference between them is precisely which events the feast commemorates. For Western Christians, the feast primarily commemorates the coming of the Magi, with only a minor reference to the baptism of Jesus and the miracle at the wedding at Cana. Eastern churches celebrate the baptism of Christ in the Jordan. In both traditions, the essence of the feast is the same: the manifestation of Christ to the world (whether as an infant or in the Jordan), and the Mystery of the [Incarnation](/wiki/Incarnation "Incarnation"). The miracle at the wedding at Cana is also celebrated during Epiphany as a first manifestation of Christ's public life.[[54]](#cite_note-BBCepiphany-54)

In some Churches, the feast of the Epiphany initiates the [Epiphany season](/wiki/Epiphany_season "Epiphany season"), also known as Epiphanytide.

### Catholic Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_(holiday)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Catholic Church")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Giotto_-_Scrovegni_-_-23-_-_Baptism_of_Christ.jpg/250px-Giotto_-_Scrovegni_-_-23-_-_Baptism_of_Christ.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giotto_-_Scrovegni_-_-23-_-_Baptism_of_Christ.jpg)

*Baptism of Christ* [fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") by [Giotto di Bondone](/wiki/Giotto_di_Bondone "Giotto di Bondone"), c. 1305 ([Cappella Scrovegni](/wiki/Cappella_Scrovegni "Cappella Scrovegni"), [Padua](/wiki/Padua "Padua"), Italy)

In the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), Epiphany is either observed on the traditional fixed date of January 6, or it is transferred to the Sunday between January 2 and January 8. Most dioceses in the United States observe the latter. In each system, the date of Epiphany affects the dates of other feasts:[[55]](#cite_note-55)

* Where Epiphany is always celebrated on January 6, the [Feast of the Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Baptism_of_the_Lord "Feast of the Baptism of the Lord") is celebrated the following Sunday. During years where there are two Sundays between Christmas and Epiphany, the second is known as the "Second Sunday after Christmas".
* Where Epiphany is on a moveable date and does not occur on January 6, it is moved earlier to replace the Second Sunday after Christmas in years where it would have occurred; otherwise it is moved later to the following Sunday, and displaces the Feast of the Baptism to the following Monday.

Currently, Epiphany is considered part of Christmas Time, which runs from First Vespers (Evening Prayer I) of Christmas up to and including the Sunday after Epiphany,[[53]](#cite_note-UNLY33-53) with [Ordinary Time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time") beginning on the following Monday.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Some regions and especially some communities celebrating the [Tridentine Mass](/wiki/Tridentine_Mass "Tridentine Mass") extend the season to as many as forty days, ending Christmastide traditionally on [Candlemas](/wiki/Candlemas "Candlemas") (February 2).

On the Feast of the Epiphany in some parts of central Europe the priest, wearing white vestments, blesses Epiphany water, [frankincense](/wiki/Frankincense "Frankincense"), gold, and chalk. The chalk is used to write the initials of the three [magi](/wiki/Magi "Magi") (traditionally, [Caspar](/wiki/Saint_Caspar "Saint Caspar"), [Melchior](/wiki/Melchior_(Magi) "Melchior (Magi)"), and [Balthasar](/wiki/Balthazar_(Magus) "Balthazar (Magus)")), over the doors of churches and homes. The initials may also be interpreted as the Latin phrase, *Christus mansionem benedicat* (may Christ bless the house).

According to ancient custom, the priest [announced](/wiki/Noveritis "Noveritis") the [date of Easter](/wiki/Computus "Computus") on the feast of Epiphany. This tradition dated from a time when calendars were not readily available, and the church needed to publicize the date of [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), since many celebrations of the [liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") depend on it.[[57]](#cite_note-Missal-57) The proclamation may be sung or proclaimed at the *[ambo](/wiki/Ambon_(liturgy) "Ambon (liturgy)")* by a [deacon](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon"), [cantor](/wiki/Cantor_(church) "Cantor (church)"), or [reader](/wiki/Reader_(liturgy) "Reader (liturgy)") either after the reading of the [Gospel](/wiki/Gospel_(liturgy) "Gospel (liturgy)") or after the [postcommunion prayer](/wiki/Postcommunion "Postcommunion").[[57]](#cite_note-Missal-57)

The [Roman Missal](/wiki/Roman_Missal "Roman Missal") thus provides a formula with appropriate chant (in the tone of the *[Exsultet](/wiki/Exsultet "Exsultet")*) for proclaiming on Epiphany, wherever it is customary to do so, the dates in the calendar for the celebration of [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday"), [Easter Sunday](/wiki/Easter_Sunday "Easter Sunday"), [Ascension of Jesus Christ](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus_Christ "Ascension of Jesus Christ"), [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"), [the Body and Blood of Christ](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_(feast) "Corpus Christi (feast)"), and the First Sunday of [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") that will mark the following liturgical year.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

### Protestant churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_(holiday)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Protestant churches")]

[Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican"), [Moravian](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") and [United Protestant](/wiki/United_Protestant "United Protestant") congregations, along with those of other denominations, may celebrate Epiphany on January 6, on the following Sunday within the Epiphany week ([octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgical) "Octave (liturgical)")), or at another time (Epiphany Eve January 5, the nearest Sunday, etc.) as local custom dictates.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) Many in the West, such as adherents of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), [Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Lutheran_Church "Lutheran Church") and [Methodist Churches](/wiki/Methodist_Church "Methodist Church"), observe a twelve-day festival, starting on December 25, and ending on January 5, known as [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide") or [The Twelve Days of Christmas](/wiki/Twelve_Days_of_Christmas "Twelve Days of Christmas"), which the Epiphany season follows.

Today, *The Epiphany of our Lord*,[[61]](#cite_note-prayerbook.ca-61) classified as a [Principal Feast](/wiki/Principal_Feast "Principal Feast"), is observed in some Anglican provinces on January 6 exclusively (e.g., the [Anglican Church of Canada](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Canada "Anglican Church of Canada"))[[61]](#cite_note-prayerbook.ca-61) but in the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") the celebration is "on 6 January or transferred to the Sunday falling between 2 and 8 January".[[62]](#cite_note-62) In Advent 2000, the Church of England, [mother church](/wiki/Mother_church "Mother church") of the Anglican Communion, introduced into its liturgy an optional Epiphany season by approving the *[Common Worship](/wiki/Common_Worship "Common Worship")* series of services as an alternative to those in the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")*, which remains the Church's normative liturgy and in which no such liturgical season appears. An official publication of the Church of England states: "The Christmas season is often celebrated for [twelve days](/wiki/Twelve_Days_of_Christmas "Twelve Days of Christmas"), ending with the Epiphany. Contemporary use has sought to express an alternative tradition, in which Christmas lasts for a full forty days, ending with the Feast of the Presentation on 2 February."[[63]](#cite_note-63) It presents the latter part of this period as the Epiphany season, comprising the Sundays of Epiphany and ending "only with the Feast of the Presentation (Candlemas)".[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Another interpretation of *Epiphany season* applies the term to the period from Epiphany to the day before [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday"). Some Methodists in the United States and Singapore follow these liturgies.[[10]](#cite_note-UMPH-10)[[65]](#cite_note-65) Lutherans celebrate the last Sunday before Ash Wednesday as the Transfiguration of our Lord, and it has been said that they call the whole period from Epiphany to then as Epiphany season.[[66]](#cite_note-66) The [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America") used the terms "Time after Epiphany" to refer to this period.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The expression with "after" has been interpreted as making the period in question correspond to that of [Ordinary Time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time").[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)

The [Presbyterian Church (USA)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)") does not celebrate Epiphany or Pentecost as seasons; for this Church, expressions such as *Fifth Sunday after Epiphany* indicate the passing of time, rather than a liturgical season. It instead uses the term *Ordinary Time*.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Some [Western rite](/wiki/Western_rite "Western rite") churches in the Protestant tradition, such as the Lutheran and Anglican churches, follow practices similar to the Catholic Church. [Church cantatas for the Feast of Epiphany](/wiki/List_of_church_cantatas_by_liturgical_occasion#Epiphany_(6_January) "List of church cantatas by liturgical occasion") were written by Protestant composers such as [Georg Philipp Telemann](/wiki/Georg_Philipp_Telemann "Georg Philipp Telemann"), [Christoph Graupner](/wiki/Christoph_Graupner "Christoph Graupner"), [Johann Sebastian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach") and [Gottfried Heinrich Stölzel](/wiki/Gottfried_Heinrich_St%C3%B6lzel "Gottfried Heinrich Stölzel").[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74)

### Eastern Orthodox churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_(holiday)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodox churches")]

Main article: [Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Baptism_of_the_Lord "Baptism of the Lord")

See also: [Tabor light](/wiki/Tabor_light "Tabor light") and [theoria](/wiki/Theoria "Theoria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Baptism_%28Kirillo-Belozersk%29.jpg/250px-Baptism_%28Kirillo-Belozersk%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baptism_(Kirillo-Belozersk).jpg)

[Russian icon](/wiki/Russian_icons "Russian icons") of the Theophany ([Kirillo-Belozersky Monastery](/wiki/Kirillo-Belozersky_Monastery "Kirillo-Belozersky Monastery"), 1497)

Eastern churches celebrate Epiphany (Theophany) on January 6. Some, as in Greece, employ the [Revised Julian calendar](/wiki/Revised_Julian_calendar "Revised Julian calendar"), which until 2800 coincides with the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar"), the one in use for civil purposes in most countries. Other Eastern churches, as in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), hold to the older [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar") for reckoning church dates. In these old-calendar churches Epiphany falls at present on Gregorian January 19 – which is January 6 in the Julian calendar.

The name of the feast as celebrated in the Orthodox churches may be rendered in English as the [Theophany](/wiki/Theophany "Theophany"), as closer in form to the [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") Θεοφάνια ("God shining forth" or "divine manifestation"). Here it is one of the [Great Feasts](/wiki/Great_Feasts "Great Feasts") of the [liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year"), being third in rank, behind only [Paskha](/wiki/Easter "Easter") (Easter) and [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") in importance. It is celebrated on January 6 of the calendar that a particular Church uses. On the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"), which some of the Orthodox churches follow, that date corresponds, during the present century, to January 19 on the [Gregorian](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar") or [Revised Julian calendar](/wiki/Revised_Julian_calendar "Revised Julian calendar"). The earliest reference to the feast in the Eastern Church is a remark by [St. Clement of Alexandria](/wiki/Clement_of_Alexandria "Clement of Alexandria") in *[Stromateis](/wiki/Stromata "Stromata")*, I, xxi, 45:

> And there are those who have determined not only the year of our Lord's birth, but also the day… And the followers of [Basilides](/wiki/Basilides "Basilides") hold the day of his baptism as a festival, spending the night before in readings. And they say that it was the fifteenth year of [Tiberius Caesar](/wiki/Tiberius_Caesar "Tiberius Caesar"), the fifteenth day of the [month of Tubi](/wiki/Month_of_Tubi "Month of Tubi"); and some that it was the eleventh of the same month.

(11 and 15 of Tubi are January 6 and 10, respectively.)

If this is a reference to a celebration of Christ's birth, as well as of his baptism, on January 6, it corresponds to what continues to be the custom of the [Armenian Apostolic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church"), which celebrates the birth of Jesus on January 6 of the calendar used, calling the feast that of the Nativity and Theophany of Our Lord.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

[Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen")'s list of festivals (in *[Contra Celsum](/wiki/Contra_Celsum "Contra Celsum")*, VIII, xxii) omits any reference to Epiphany. The first reference to an ecclesiastical feast of the Epiphany, in [Ammianus Marcellinus](/wiki/Ammianus_Marcellinus "Ammianus Marcellinus") (XXI:ii), is in 361.

In parts of the Eastern Church, January 6 continued for some time as a composite feast that included the Nativity of Jesus: though Constantinople adopted December 25 to commemorate Jesus' birth in the fourth century, in other parts the Nativity of Jesus continued to be celebrated on January 6, a date later devoted exclusively to commemorating his Baptism.[[41]](#cite_note-Pokhilko-41)

Today in [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") churches, the emphasis at this feast is on the shining forth and revelation of Jesus Christ as the [Messiah](/wiki/Messiah "Messiah") and [Second Person](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son") of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") at the time of his baptism. It is also celebrated because, according to tradition, the [baptism of Jesus](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus") in the [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River") by [St. John the Baptist](/wiki/St._John_the_Baptist "St. John the Baptist") marked one of only two occasions when all three Persons of the Trinity manifested themselves simultaneously to humanity: [God the Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father") by speaking through the clouds, [God the Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son") being baptized in the river, and [God the Holy Spirit](/wiki/God_the_Holy_Spirit "God the Holy Spirit") in the shape of a dove descending from heaven (the other occasion was the [Transfiguration](/wiki/Transfiguration_of_Jesus "Transfiguration of Jesus") on [Mount Tabor](/wiki/Mount_Tabor "Mount Tabor")). Thus the holy day is considered to be a Trinitarian feast.

The Orthodox consider Jesus' Baptism to be the first step towards the [Crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), and there are some parallels in the hymnography used on this day and the hymns chanted on [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday").

#### Liturgical practice in Eastern churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_(holiday)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Liturgical practice in Eastern churches")]

*Forefeast*: The liturgical [Forefeast](/wiki/Forefeast "Forefeast") of Theophany begins on January 2[[77]](#cite_note-77) and concludes with the Paramony on January 5.

*Paramony*: The [Eve](/wiki/Eve "Eve") of the Feast is called *Paramony* ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): παραμονή, [Slavonic](/wiki/Church_Slavonic "Church Slavonic"): *navechérie*). Paramony is observed as a strict [fast day](/wiki/Fasting#Eastern_Orthodoxy "Fasting"), on which those faithful who are physically able, refrain from food until the first star is observed in the evening, when a meal with wine and oil may be taken. On this day the [Royal Hours](/wiki/Royal_Hours "Royal Hours") are celebrated, thus tying together this feast with [Nativity](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") and Good Friday. The Royal Hours are followed by the [Divine Liturgy of St. Basil](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy_of_St._Basil "Divine Liturgy of St. Basil") which combines [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") with the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy"). During the Vespers, fifteen [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") [lections](/wiki/Lection "Lection") which foreshadow the Baptism of Christ are read, and special [antiphons](/wiki/Antiphon "Antiphon") are chanted. If the Feast of the Theophany falls on a Sunday or Monday, the Royal Hours are chanted on the previous Friday, and on the Paramony the [Divine Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy_of_St._John_Chrysostom "Divine Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom") is celebrated in the morning. The fasting is lessened to some degree in this case.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Theophany_Crucession_in_Bulgaria.jpg/250px-Theophany_Crucession_in_Bulgaria.jpg)](/wiki/File:Theophany_Crucession_in_Bulgaria.jpg)

Theophany [Crucession](/wiki/Crucession "Crucession") in [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgarian_Orthodox_Church "Bulgarian Orthodox Church"). The priests are going to throw a wooden cross in the [Yantra river](/wiki/Yantra_(river) "Yantra (river)"). Believers will then jump into the icy waters to recover the cross.

*Blessing of Waters*: The Orthodox Churches perform the [Great Blessing of Waters](/wiki/Great_Blessing_of_Waters "Great Blessing of Waters") on Theophany.[[78]](#cite_note-78) The blessing is normally done twice: once on the Eve of the Feast – usually at a [Baptismal font](/wiki/Baptismal_font "Baptismal font") inside the church – and then again on the day of the feast, outdoors at a body of water. Following the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy"), the clergy and people go in a [Crucession](/wiki/Crucession "Crucession") (procession with the cross) to the nearest body of water, be it a beach, harbor, quay, river, lake, swimming pool, water depot, etc. (ideally, it should be a body of "living water"). At the end of the ceremony the priest will bless the waters. In the Greek practice, he does this by casting a cross into the water. If swimming is feasible on the spot, any number of volunteers may try to recover the cross. The person who gets the cross first swims back and returns it to the priest, who then delivers a special blessing to the swimmer and their household. Certain such ceremonies have achieved particular prominence, such as the one held annually at [Tarpon Springs](/wiki/Tarpon_Springs,_Florida "Tarpon Springs, Florida"), Florida. In Russia, where the winters are severe, a hole will be cut into the ice so that the waters may be blessed. In such conditions, the cross is not cast into the water, but is held securely by the priest and dipped three times into the water.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Cross_being_thrown_at_Theophany.jpg/250px-Cross_being_thrown_at_Theophany.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cross_being_thrown_at_Theophany.jpg)

[Greek Orthodox](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox "Greek Orthodox") [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") at the [Great Blessing of Waters](/wiki/Great_Blessing_of_Waters "Great Blessing of Waters") on Theophany, releasing the cross off the [Glenelg](/wiki/Glenelg,_South_Australia "Glenelg, South Australia") Jetty, [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia"), for one of the swimmers below to retrieve

The water that is blessed on this day is sometimes known as "Theophany Water", though usually just "holy water", and is taken home by the faithful, and used with prayer as a blessing. People will not only bless themselves and their homes by sprinkling with holy water, but will also drink it. The Orthodox Church teaches that holy water differs from ordinary water by virtue of the incorruptibility bestowed upon it by a blessing that transforms its very nature.[[79]](#cite_note-79) a miracle attested to as early as [St. John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom").[[80]](#cite_note-80)

Theophany is a traditional day for performing [Baptisms](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"), and this is reflected in the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") by singing the baptismal hymn, "As many as have been baptized into Christ, have put on Christ. Alleluia," in place of the [Trisagion](/wiki/Trisagion "Trisagion").

*House Blessings*: On Theophany the priest will begin making the round of the parishioner's homes to bless them. He will perform a short prayer service in each home, and then go through the entire house, gardens and outside-buildings, blessing them with the newly blessed Theophany Water, while all sing the [Troparion](/wiki/Troparion "Troparion") and [Kontakion](/wiki/Kontakion "Kontakion") of the feast. This is normally done on Theophany, or at least during the [Afterfeast](/wiki/Afterfeast "Afterfeast"), but if the parishioners are numerous, and especially if many live far away from the church, it may take some time to bless each house. Traditionally, these blessings should all be finished before the beginning of [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent").

*Afterfeast*: The Feast of Theophany is followed by an eight-day [Afterfeast](/wiki/Afterfeast "Afterfeast") on which the normal fasting laws are suspended. The Saturday and Sunday after Theophany have special readings assigned to them, which relate to the [Temptation of Christ](/wiki/Temptation_of_Jesus "Temptation of Jesus") and to penance and perseverance in the Christian struggle. There is thus a liturgical continuum between the Feast of Theophany and the beginning of Great Lent.

### Oriental Orthodox

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_(holiday)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Oriental Orthodox")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Pindi_Perunnal_2019_In_Kunnamkulam_04.jpg/250px-Pindi_Perunnal_2019_In_Kunnamkulam_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pindi_Perunnal_2019_In_Kunnamkulam_04.jpg)

Epiphany in [Kerala](/wiki/Kerala "Kerala"), India

In the [Ethiopian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Ethiopian_Orthodox_Church "Ethiopian Orthodox Church"), the feast is known as *[Timkat](/wiki/Timkat "Timkat")* and is celebrated on the day that the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar") calls January 19, but on January 20 in years when [Enkutatash](/wiki/Enkutatash "Enkutatash") in the [Ethiopian calendar](/wiki/Ethiopian_calendar "Ethiopian calendar") falls on Gregorian September 12 (i.e. when the following February in the Gregorian calendar will have 29 days). The celebration of this feast features blessing of water and solemn processions with the sacred [tabot](/wiki/Tabot "Tabot"). A priest carries this to a body of water where it stays overnight, with the [Metsehafe Qeddassie](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") celebrated in the early morning. Later in the morning, the water is blessed to the accompaniment of the reading of the four Gospel accounts of the baptism of Jesus in the Jordan and the people are sprinkled with or go into the water. The tabot returns in procession to the church[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

Among the [Syriac Christians](/wiki/Syriac_Christians "Syriac Christians") the feast is called *denho* (up-going), a name to be connected with the notion of rising light expressed in [Luke 1:78](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Luke%201:78&version=nrsv). In the [East Syriac rite](/wiki/East_Syriac_rite "East Syriac rite"), the season of Epiphany (Epiphanytide) is known as *Denha*[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

In the [Armenian Apostolic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church"), January 6 is celebrated as the Nativity (*[Soorp Tsnund](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D5%8D%D5%B8%D6%82%D6%80%D5%A2_%D4%BE%D5%B6%D5%B8%D6%82%D5%B6%D5%A4 "wikt:Սուրբ Ծնունդ")*) and Theophany of Christ. The feast is preceded by a seven-day fast. On the eve of the feast, the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") is celebrated. This liturgy is referred to as the *[Chragaluytsi](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D5%B3%D6%80%D5%A1%D5%A3%D5%A1%D5%AC%D5%B8%D6%82%D5%B5%D6%81 "wikt:ճրագալույց") [Patarag](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D5%BA%D5%A1%D5%BF%D5%A1%D6%80%D5%A1%D5%A3 "wikt:պատարագ")* (the Eucharist of the lighting of the lamps) in honor of the manifestation of Jesus as the Son of God. Both the Armenian Apostolic Church's and [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East")'s liturgy is followed by a blessing of water, during which the cross is immersed in the water, symbolizing Jesus' descent into the Jordan, and holy *myron* ([chrism](/wiki/Chrism "Chrism")) is poured in, symbolic of the descent of the Holy Spirit upon Jesus. The next morning, after the Liturgy, the cross is removed from the vessel of holy water and all come forward to kiss the cross and partake of the blessed water[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

The [Indian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Indian_Orthodox_Church "Indian Orthodox Church") celebrates the feast of Epiphany, *Denaha* [Syriac term which means rising] on January 6, and the Ethiopian Orthodox Church celebrates on January 19 as the Timkath festival, which was included in the UNESCO heritage list of festivals[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*].

## Music
